mnemonics.title: Time Series Components
mnemonics.content: TSCR - Trend, Seasonality, Cyclical, Random (Think: 'Tired Squirrel Chewing Raisins')

mnemonics.title: Exponential Smoothing Weights
mnemonics.content: Recent data gets more weight because 'New Is Better' (New = higher weight)

mnemonics.title: Forecasting Accuracy
mnemonics.content: MAPE - Mean Absolute Percentage Error: Think 'MApes up = Bad, MApes down = Good'

acronyms.usage: Smoothing time series data by averaging last k observations with equal weights
Smoothing with higher weights on recent data for faster trend response
Standard metric to measure forecast accuracy as percentage error
Metric that penalizes large errors heavily (squares the errors)
Average absolute difference between forecast and actual values
Square root of MSE, in same units as original data
Advanced time series forecasting combining autoregression, differencing, and moving average

visualTips.visualization: Draw a straight line through scattered data points - if upward slope = positive trend, downward = negative trend, horizontal = no trend
Picture a wave pattern repeating at regular intervals like waves on a beach (daily/weekly/yearly cycles)
Imagine a triangle or pyramid where the peak (recent data) is highest and weights decrease going backward
Think of a layered cake - bottom layer = trend, middle = seasonality, top = random noise, stacked to form the original series

analogies.analogy: Moving average is like the moving average temperature of the last 7 days - it smooths daily fluctuations to show the underlying weather pattern
Exponential smoothing is like a moving average but 'with memory' - it remembers all past values but gives more importance to recent conversations in a discussion
If you're climbing a mountain: the gradual ascent is the trend, the daily rest stops at camps are seasonality, unexpected storms are random variations
MAPE is like your GPS showing average percentage distance error from your actual destination - lower percentage means more accurate navigation
